issues.body: The `pred_stat` fallback only returns a predicate NDV when the opposite terminal is unbound ((0,..) requires id_pat[2].is_none(); (2,..) requires id_pat[0].is_none()), otherwise returning `est` because the column is effectively keyed. The new branch (exec.rs:7618-7621) returns the catalog NDV for any pos in {0,2} regardless of whether the other terminal is bound. It is clamped to [1.0, est] and only feeds cost estimation, so query results are unaffected, but for keyed lookups the feature yields a different (arguably worse) estimate than the documented fallback in exactly the cases the fallback deliberately treats as keyed. Unchanged since round 1.
issues.fix_hint: Mirror the fallback's guards before consulting the catalog: only take the catalog NDV when the opposite terminal is unbound (subject-var + id_pat[2].is_none() for pos 0; object-var + id_pat[0].is_none() for pos 2); otherwise fall through so keyed columns still estimate `est`.
issues.title: persistent-stats branch ignores the 'other-terminal-bound -> keyed' guard the fallback applies

issues.body: `predicate_ndv` computes `hist.iter().map(|b| b.distinct).sum()`, which always equals the true NDV (freq.len()) because every freq entry increments `distinct` exactly once and each is flushed into exactly one bucket. The per-bucket `upper`/`count` and the equi-depth boundaries are never read by the only consumer, so the histogram machinery is currently forward-looking storage rather than an active planning input. This is correct (the returned value is an exact NDV) but the equi-depth buckets earn no keep for v1. Unchanged since round 1.
issues.fix_hint: Either wire a histogram-consuming estimate (range/point selectivity for a bound terminal) so the buckets earn their keep, or store just the per-column distinct count for v1 and add histograms when a consumer lands. If keeping the buckets, add a short comment noting the per-bucket detail is intentionally unused so far.
issues.title: Only the summed histogram `distinct` is consumed; equi-depth bucket detail is dead for the estimator

issues.body: `with_stats_catalog` installs a catalog on the thread and `predicate_ndv` looks it up by dictionary id, but dictionary ids are graph-specific. Installing a catalog analyzed for graph A while querying graph B returns A's NDV for any colliding predicate id. It is planning-only so results stay correct, but it is a silent footgun with no validation. Also: the catalog is read via a thread-local, so if planning ever runs off the installing thread the catalog is silently ignored (feature no-ops) with no diagnostic. Unchanged since round 1.
issues.fix_hint: Persist a cheap graph fingerprint (dict length / store epoch) in the header and validate it in load/with_stats_catalog, or document the caller's obligation to install only a catalog analyzed from the same saved store, plus the on-thread-planning requirement, in the module and SKILL doc.
issues.title: No catalog<->graph identity binding; a mismatched catalog silently feeds foreign estimates

issues.body: `analyze_round_trips_deterministically_and_rejects_corruption` appends a trailing byte and asserts 'mutation must be detected'. The format has no checksum; detection here comes solely from the exact-length `if at != bytes.len()` trailing-data check. An in-place value flip (e.g. mutating a bucket `count`) that preserves length would decode successfully and go undetected, so the comment reads as a stronger integrity claim than the format provides. Fine for a planning-only catalog. Unchanged since round 1.
issues.fix_hint: Reword to 'trailing/truncated data must be rejected', or add an in-place byte-flip case (and, if in-place detection is actually desired, a checksum) so the assertion matches the claim.
issues.title: Corruption test comment overstates the format's integrity guarantee

issues.body: Both feature-ON tests build a temp dir from `std::env::temp_dir().join(format!("...{}", std::process::id()))` with no random/unique suffix; a stale dir from a crashed prior run (reused pid) could interfere, and the dir is only removed on the success path (a panic leaks it). Separately, `analyze` accumulates a full BTreeMap of every subject and object per predicate before histogramming (O(triples) memory for an offline op) — acceptable for v1 but worth noting for large stores. Unchanged since round 1.
issues.fix_hint: Use a unique temp dir (tempfile or a per-test atomic counter) and clean it even on panic; consider a streaming/bounded accumulation for `analyze` or document the memory profile.
issues.title: Test temp dirs keyed only by process id; analyze holds full per-predicate value distribution in memory

verdict.summary: Opt-in `persistent-stats` feature: an explicitly-built `stats.bin` catalog (per-predicate cardinalities + equi-depth subject/object id histograms) written atomically (tmp+fsync+rename) beside a saved store, installed via a thread-local guard and consumed only by `pattern_var_ndv` as an NDV estimate clamped to [1.0, est]. It is a pure planning/selectivity input — it can reorder joins but never change query results (verified: the exec.rs branch is `#[cfg(feature="persistent-stats")]`-gated, so the default native + wasm builds compile it out entirely). Confirmed the feature-ON test APIs all exist in-tree: `Graph::load_str`/`iter_ids`, `store.pred_stat`, `Id::{from,to}_le_bytes`, `crate::query`, and `QueryResult{vars,rows}`. decode() is fully bounds-checked (checked take, per-record/per-bucket count caps against remaining input, exact-length trailing check) with no panic or unbounded allocation on hostile bytes. The round-2 delta over round 1 is the addition of the `sparq-engine (persistent-stats)` CI leg (feature-matrix.d + golden legnames, alphabetically consistent), which now genuinely exercises the feature-ON module (round-trip, corruption-rejection, deterministic-encode, and a non-vacuous result-equivalence differential with a decline path). No blocker or major issue. The findings below are the same planning-only quality/doc nuances recorded in round 1; none were changed by this round and none block merge.
